text: The surgeon begins the procedure by freeing the right and left crura of the diaphragm, followed by dissection of the esophagus.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the intra-abdominal and distal intrathoracic esophagus. Following endoscopic surveillance of the area with high-grade dysplasia, the team proceeds with a laparoscopic distal esophagectomy.
The surgeon performs complete devascularization of the intrathoracic esophagus and proximal stomach, carefully severing blood vessels to isolate these structures.
The surgeon widely opens the diaphragmatic crura to enhance the laparoscopic view, providing better access to the esophagus.
The surgeon uses linear staplers to divide the distal esophagus and proximal stomach, preparing for esophageal jejunostomy to prevent reflux.
The surgeon preserves the stomach in its distal, proximal, and mid portions, ensuring these areas remain intact for potential future use in a gastric pull-up procedure.
The surgeon uses an endoscope to verify the positioning of the dysplastic area before transecting the stomach at the distal thoracic portion.
The surgeon constructs a Roux limb and brings it up to the abdomen in an anticholic, anti-fashion orientation.
The surgeon performs an enterotomy and esophagotomy, followed by a side-to-side esophago-jejunostomy using a blue cartridge stapler.

The surgeon encloses the esophago-jejunostomy site using a running double layer of varical sutures, completing the anastomosis with visible precision. This technique is performed outside the chest cavity to prevent acid reflux and recurrence of dysplasia.
The surgeon checks the esophago-jejunostomy site for leakage using arometinol blue dye. The 'candy cane' aspect of the esophago-jejunostomy is visually secured to the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon reconstructs the lower part of the proximal gastrointestinal tract by performing a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy between the alimentary and biliopancreatic limbs.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler with two applications of a white cartridge to close the jejuno-jejunostomy side in a linear fashion.
A crown of clips is placed around the esophago-jejunostomy site to mark the anastomosis for identification in future x-rays.
The surgeon places drains in the cytopatic space, ensuring proper fluid management and preventing accumulation in the surgical area.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a protective bag to prevent tumor cell implantation during removal.
